facts.text: Llewellyn Park was founded in 1853 by Llewellyn Solomon Haskell, a New York City businessman, and designed by Alexander Jackson Davis.
The park’s Gatehouse, once home to a caretaker and the park’s team of horses, now houses the Llewellyn Park Historical Society archives.
Llewellyn Park is considered the country’s first planned residential community, featuring winding paths and ornamental trees in a 19th‑century romantic style.
The Llewellyn Park Historic District entered the National Register of Historic Places in 1985, preserving its unique 19th‑century landscape design.
Thomas Edison’s Glenmont estate, located within Llewellyn Park, is part of the Thomas Edison National Historical Park today.
The first annual meeting of Llewellyn Park proprietors convened at the Gatehouse on January 1, 1858, a tradition that continues today.
